sections.header: Verses 1–10 — “The Rabshakeh’s Challenge: On Whom Are You Depending?”

heb.paragraph: "On what are you basing this confidence (bāṭaḥ) of yours?" (v.4). Historical narrative begins: 701 BC. Sennacherib of Assyria invades Judah, captures all the fortified cities, and sends his field commander (Rabshakeh) to Jerusalem (v.2). The Rabshakeh speaks in Hebrew to the defenders on the wall (v.11). His argument: (1) Egypt is a broken reed (v.6). (2) You’ve removed your own altars (v.7) — misunderstanding Hezekiah’s reforms. (3) Even the LORD told me to attack (v.10). Psychological warfare through theological manipulation.

notes.note: "In the fourteenth year of King Hezekiah, Sennacherib attacked all the fortified cities of Judah and captured them." Parallel: 2 Kings 18:13–37. Isaiah shifts from poetry to prose. The test of everything he’s preached.

"On whom are you depending? Egypt is a splintered reed of a staff that pierces the hand of anyone who leans on it." The Rabshakeh uses Isaiah’s own argument (chs.30–31): Egypt is useless. The enemy quotes the prophet.
"The LORD himself told me to march against this country and destroy it." The Rabshakeh claims divine authorisation. The theological audacity: a pagan officer claiming YHWH’s mandate.

refs.note: The parallel account includes Hezekiah's initial capitulation and tribute payment, which Isaiah omits to focus on the theological confrontation.
Chronicles adds Hezekiah's fortification efforts and his speech rallying Jerusalem, emphasizing faithful preparation.
The psalm's confidence that God is refuge even when earth gives way resonates with Jerusalem's situation under siege.

hist.context: In 701 BC, Sennacherib of Assyria invaded Judah after Hezekiah joined an anti-Assyrian coalition. The Rabshakeh (chief cupbearer) was a high official sent to negotiate Jerusalem's surrender. His speech, delivered in Hebrew rather than Aramaic, was psychological warfare aimed at undermining civilian morale. The mention of Egypt as a 'splintered reed' reflects the failed Egyptian alliance that had encouraged Judah's rebellion.

sections.header: Verses 11–22 — “Has Any God Delivered His Land from the King of Assyria?”

heb.paragraph: "Has the god of any nation ever delivered (hiṣṣîl) his land from the hand of the king of Assyria?" (v.18). Hezekiah’s officials beg: speak in Aramaic, not Hebrew — don’t let the people on the wall hear (v.11). The Rabshakeh deliberately raises his voice (v.13). His pitch to the common people: make peace with me, enjoy your own vines and fig trees (v.16). Then the theological challenge: no god has saved anyone from Assyria (vv.18–20). "How then can the LORD deliver Jerusalem?" (v.20). The people are silent — the king had commanded them not to answer (v.21). Hezekiah’s officials tear their clothes (v.22).

notes.note: "Please speak in Aramaic, not Hebrew!" The officials want diplomatic language; the Rabshakeh wants propaganda. He shouts louder. The language choice is a weapon.

"Has any god of any nation delivered his land from the king of Assyria? How then can the LORD deliver Jerusalem?" The Rabshakeh’s fatal error: equating YHWH with the gods of Hamath and Arpad. This is the challenge Isaiah has prepared Hezekiah to answer.

"The people remained silent. They tore their clothes." Silence and torn clothes: the posture of crisis. The silence is obedient (the king commanded it) and faithful (they don’t negotiate with the enemy).

refs.note: The parallel preserves identical dialogue, confirming this account's historical reliability through independent transmission.
The Rabshakeh's taunt 'where are their gods?' inverts Moses's Song, which mocks idols that cannot save.
Earlier Isaiah had called Assyria God's rod of judgment; now Assyria overreaches by claiming credit for victories God ordained.

hist.context: The Rabshakeh's demand that officials speak Aramaic (the diplomatic lingua franca) rather than Hebrew reveals the propaganda dimension of the confrontation. Assyrian royal inscriptions boasted of making conquered peoples' gods powerless; the Rabshakeh applies this template to YHWH. The officials' silence and torn clothes signal both grief and the gravity of blasphemy against Israel's God.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVEliakim son of Hilkiah the palace administrator, Shebna the secretary, and Joah son of Asaph the recorder went out to him.ESVAnd there came out to him Eliakim the son of Hilkiah, who was over the household, and Shebna the secretary, and Joah the son of Asaph, the recorder.NIVThen the king of Assyria sent his field commander with a large army from Lachish to King Hezekiah at Jerusalem. When the commander stopped at the aqueduct of the Upper Pool, on the road to the Launderer’s Field,ESVAnd the king of Assyria sent the Rabshakeh from Lachish to King Hezekiah at Jerusalem, with a great army. And he stood by the conduit of the upper pool on the highway to the Washer’s Field.

rows.label: No God Has Delivered — 36:11–22
rows.text: "Has ANY GOD delivered his land from Assyria?" The theological gauntlet. The people are SILENT. Clothes torn.

hebtext.note: "On what are you basing this confidence (bāṭaḥ) of yours?" (v.4). Historical narrative begins: 701 BC. Sennacherib of Assyria invades Judah, captures all the fortified cities, and sends his field commander (Rabshakeh) to Jerusalem (v.2). The Rabshakeh speaks in Hebrew to the defenders on the wall (v.11). His argument: (1) Egypt is a broken reed (v.6). (2) You’ve removed your own altars (v.7) — misunderstanding Hezekiah’s reforms. (3) Even the LORD told me to attack (v.10). Psychological warfare through theological manipulation.

"Has the god of any nation ever delivered (hiṣṣîl) his land from the hand of the king of Assyria?" (v.18). Hezekiah’s officials beg: speak in Aramaic, not Hebrew — don’t let the people on the wall hear (v.11). The Rabshakeh deliberately raises his voice (v.13). His pitch to the common people: make peace with me, enjoy your own vines and fig trees (v.16). Then the theological challenge: no god has saved anyone from Assyria (vv.18–20). "How then can the LORD deliver Jerusalem?" (v.20). The people are silent — the king had commanded them not to answer (v.21). Hezekiah’s officials tear their clothes (v.22).

themes.note: Faith peaks: the theological crisis. Everything Isaiah preached about trusting God, not Egypt, is now tested at the wall. The Rabshakeh’s challenge is the exam.
